title: 📝 Mock Exam C — Full paper (exam-style)
blurb: A complete timed-style mock exam in the professor's format: mostly concept short-answers, one Keras CNN code question, and a CNN calculation. Covers the whole syllabus with emphasis on NN fundamentals + CNN.

knowledge_points.title: Part 1 — NN & CNN core
knowledge_points.recap: Exam-style questions covering this section — attempt each, then reveal the model answer.

questions.q: **Activation functions.** (a) Why does a neural network need a **non-linear** activation function at all — what happens if every layer uses only linear activations? (b) Give one concrete advantage of **ReLU** over the **sigmoid**. (c) Explain the **dying ReLU** problem and name one variant that mitigates it.
questions.answer: (a) Without non-linearity, a stack of linear layers is mathematically equivalent to a **single linear transformation** ($W_2(W_1x)=Wx$), so the network — no matter how deep — can only learn **linear functions** and cannot model complex/non-linear decision boundaries. (b) ReLU does not saturate for positive inputs, so it largely avoids the **vanishing-gradient** problem that sigmoid suffers (sigmoid gradients are near-zero at the tails), giving faster convergence; it is also cheaper to compute. (c) **Dying ReLU**: if a neuron's pre-activation stays negative for all inputs, its output is always 0 and its gradient is always 0, so it stops learning permanently. Mitigated by **Leaky ReLU** (or PReLU / ELU), which allows a small negative slope so the gradient is never exactly zero.

questions.q: **CNN properties.** A convolutional layer (weight sharing, sliding kernel) has a key symmetry property with respect to input shifts. Which statement is correct?
questions.answer: Convolution is **translation-equivariant**: shift the input and the feature map shifts by the same amount. Full translation **invariance** (output unchanged by shifts) is only approximately achieved *after* pooling / global pooling. So (a) is correct: convolution gives equivariance, and pooling adds a degree of invariance.

options.text: Convolution is translation-equivariant; pooling then introduces a degree of translation invariance.
Convolution is fully translation-invariant on its own, so pooling is unnecessary.
Convolution is neither equivariant nor invariant to shifts; only fully-connected layers are.
Equivariance and invariance mean the same thing for a CNN.

questions.extend: **Receptive field**: the region of the input that influences one output unit. Stacking conv layers (or using stride/dilation) enlarges the receptive field, letting deeper units 'see' more context.

questions.q: **CNN calculation.** An input image is $32\times32\times3$. It passes through a Conv2D layer with **16 filters**, kernel size $5\times5$, **stride 1**, **no padding** ('valid'), followed by a $2\times2$ **max-pooling** (stride 2). (a) What is the spatial size of the feature map *after the convolution*? (b) How many **trainable parameters** does the Conv2D layer have (include biases)? (c) What is the output size (H×W×C) *after* the pooling?
questions.answer: (a) Output side $=(32-5)/1+1=\mathbf{28}$, so the conv feature map is $\mathbf{28\times28\times16}$. (b) Params $=(5\times5\times3+1)\times16=(75+1)\times16=76\times16=\mathbf{1216}$. (c) Pooling halves each spatial dim: $28/2=14$, channels unchanged $\Rightarrow \mathbf{14\times14\times16}$.

questions.q: **Keras code.** Write a Keras (`tf.keras`) program that builds, compiles, trains and evaluates a small CNN for **10-class** image classification on $28\times28$ grayscale images. The labels are supplied as **integers** (not one-hot). Include at least one Conv2D + **BatchNormalization**, a pooling layer, a dense head, and use the loss appropriate for **integer** labels.
questions.answer:
Key points: final Dense uses **softmax** with 10 units, and because labels are integers the loss is **`sparse_categorical_crossentropy`** (not `categorical_crossentropy`). BatchNormalization is placed after the Conv layer.

```python
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras import layers, models

model = models.Sequential([
    layers.Input(shape=(28, 28, 1)),
    layers.Conv2D(32, (3, 3), activation='relu'),
    layers.BatchNormalization(),
    layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2)),
    layers.Conv2D(64, (3, 3), activation='relu'),
    layers.BatchNormalization(),
    layers.MaxPooling2D((2, 2)),
    layers.Flatten(),
    layers.Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    layers.Dense(10, activation='softmax')
])

model.compile(optimizer='adam',
              loss='sparse_categorical_crossentropy',
              metrics=['accuracy'])

model.fit(x_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32,
          validation_split=0.1)

test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(x_test, y_test)
print('Test accuracy:', test_acc)
```
questions.extend: If labels were one-hot vectors you would instead use `categorical_crossentropy`. For binary output you would use a single sigmoid unit with `binary_crossentropy`.

questions.q: **Classical ML.** (a) In one sentence, how does a **random forest** reduce the high variance of a single decision tree? (b) Which choice below best describes the **bias–variance** effect of averaging many trees?
questions.answer: (a) A random forest trains many **de-correlated** decision trees on **bootstrap** samples with **random feature subsets** at each split, then averages/votes their predictions; averaging many high-variance, low-bias trees **reduces variance** without much increase in bias. (b) The correct option is that it **lowers variance while keeping bias roughly the same**.

options.text: Averaging many de-correlated trees mainly reduces variance while bias stays roughly unchanged.
It mainly reduces bias, and variance is unaffected.
It increases both bias and variance, which is why forests overfit more than single trees.

knowledge_points.title: Part 2 — Breadth
knowledge_points.recap: Exam-style questions covering this section — attempt each, then reveal the model answer.

questions.q: **Sequences & embeddings.** (a) What problem does a word **embedding** layer solve compared with one-hot encoding of a large vocabulary? (b) Why do plain **RNNs** struggle with long sequences, and name one architecture that addresses this.
questions.answer: (a) One-hot vectors are **high-dimensional and sparse** (size = vocabulary) and carry **no notion of similarity** between words. An **embedding** maps each token to a **dense, low-dimensional, learned** vector where semantically similar words lie close together, drastically reducing dimensionality and letting the model generalize. (b) Plain RNNs suffer from **vanishing/exploding gradients** when back-propagating through many time steps, so they cannot retain **long-range dependencies**. **LSTM** (or GRU) uses gating/cell state to preserve information over long spans.

questions.q: **Attention / Transformers.** (a) In one line, what does **self-attention** compute for a token? (b) Give one advantage of the Transformer over an RNN. (c) How does a **Vision Transformer (ViT)** feed an image into a Transformer?
questions.answer: (a) For each token it computes a **weighted sum of the values** of all tokens, where the weights come from the **query·key** similarity ($\text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d})V$) — i.e. how much each other token should attend to it. (b) It processes the whole sequence **in parallel** (no sequential recurrence) and captures **long-range dependencies** directly, giving better scalability and training speed. (c) ViT splits the image into fixed-size **patches**, linearly **embeds** each patch (plus a **positional encoding**), and treats the sequence of patch-embeddings like tokens fed to a standard Transformer encoder.

questions.q: **Reinforcement learning — calculation.** A simple MDP: from state $S$ two actions are available. Action A gives an immediate reward of $5$ and leads to a terminal state (value $0$). Action B gives an immediate reward of $2$ and leads to state $S'$ whose value is $V(S')=10$. Use a **discount factor** $\gamma=0.9$. (a) Compute the Q-value $Q(S,A)$. (b) Compute $Q(S,B)$. (c) Under a greedy policy, what is $V(S)$ and which action is chosen?
questions.answer: Using $Q(s,a)=r+\gamma V(\text{next})$. (a) $Q(S,A)=5+0.9\times0=\mathbf{5}$. (b) $Q(S,B)=2+0.9\times10=2+9=\mathbf{11}$. (c) Greedy value $V(S)=\max(5,11)=\mathbf{11}$, and the agent chooses **action B**.
questions.extend: This is one backup step of **value iteration**. The **exploration–exploitation** trade-off (e.g. **epsilon-greedy**: act greedily with prob $1-\epsilon$, random with prob $\epsilon$) is what lets an agent discover that B is better before committing to it.

questions.q: **Generative AI / LLMs.** A team wants an LLM to answer questions accurately over a company's constantly-updated internal documents. (a) Contrast **RAG** (retrieval-augmented generation) with **fine-tuning** as ways to inject this knowledge. (b) Give one reason RAG is often preferred when the documents change frequently.
questions.answer: (a) **Fine-tuning** updates the model's **weights** on the company data, baking the knowledge in — expensive to retrain, and the knowledge becomes stale as soon as documents change. **RAG** keeps the base model frozen and, at query time, **retrieves** relevant document chunks from a vector store and adds them to the **prompt/context**, so the model reasons over up-to-date external text without weight changes. (b) With RAG you only **update the document index**, not the model — new/changed documents are reflected immediately, it avoids costly retraining, and retrieved sources can be **cited**, reducing hallucination. Fine-tuning better teaches *style/format or skills* rather than volatile facts.

questions.q: **ML for EDA / Agentic AI.** (a) Explain how a **surrogate (proxy) model** accelerates an EDA flow, and why this is described as turning an $O(n^2)$-style evaluation into effectively $O(1)$ at inference. (b) Name the three core components of the **Brain/Tools/Memory** loop of an **agentic AI** system and give one line on each.
questions.answer: (a) Running an exact EDA analysis (e.g. a full simulation, timing/DRC/placement evaluation) is **slow and expensive**. A **surrogate model** is a fast ML approximator trained on many prior expensive evaluations; once trained, a prediction is a single cheap **forward pass** — effectively **$O(1)$** — instead of re-running the costly analysis, so it can be called thousands of times inside an optimization loop that would otherwise be infeasible. (b) **Brain** = the LLM/planner that reasons and decides the next step; **Tools** = external actions/APIs (simulators, search, code execution) the agent can call to affect the world; **Memory** = stored context/history (short- and long-term) so the agent can recall past results and maintain state across the loop.
